children.text: Song "Pa gozar con el ritmo" by Victoria Santa Cruz

"A long, long time ago, back when I was still just a little kid, I was always hearing my aunts tell me: "Now, don't go getting involved with a Black woman, no, not with a Black woman! You have to marry a white woman so your kids turn out good-looking... and not like Machi."

This testimony by a Black man reflects on the politics of blanqueamiento, whitening, and the colonial idea that it is necessary to have lighter children in order to delimit Blackness. Additionally, the testimony claims that lighter-skinned children are considered more beautiful, which also reflects a beauty standard rooted in whiteness.

"Maybe what I am about to say can be a harsh critique of our own Black community. But I think that some of us have believed in the tale that because we have Black skin, we are less than something. It is the idea that because of our skin, we have fewer ways of thinking, but not in everything. A larger part of our community has believed that without resistance to those ideas. "
Susana reflects on the internationalization of the idea that Blackness is less than human.
Song “Lucio Cotito” playing
"Some people, they look at it, they see your art. But others... others, I think. They just see the body, or I don't know, but everyone... everyone, I believe, approaches it by looking at your skin."
Sandra reflects on how her body is reduced while she performs dances and her artwork.

"I am Negra, and mind you, "A" changes the gender. I am a woman, and being a woman entails a specific kind of struggle in relation to the broader social group, primarily because of how society as a whole perceives the term. To reach a certain level, I have to spend an immense amount of time relentlessly pushing at things until people finally grasp that I am capable of doing the job because I actually know how to do it. First, I have to make others look past the color of my skin, and second, I have to make them look past my gender. People simply refuse to accept me for who I am. That is to say, quite simply, a woman cannot give orders to a group of men."
Susana reveals the intersection of gender and race and how she has to navigate a world that reduces her to her gender and race
"When people see a white person with a Black person or a Black person with a white person, they really do turn their heads or look on in astonishment.”
Esther “Mamaine” Cartagena reflects on the racism in interracial relationships.

“Based on our own experience as a couple. The rejection we’ve felt hasn't simply been a matter of the excuse used. People point out that we aren't married, and they claim that is the reason for like a veto. But in reality, the underlying issue is racial. No one seems able to believe that ours is a relationship involving a deeper, more tender bond. one characterized by mutual understanding on a cultural level, shared interests, and common tastes. They see it as nothing more than sex. Just sex. So how do we know this? Because of how people approach us, whether they direct their attention toward Óscar or toward me, it’s always the same dynamic. The assumption is either that I picked him up, or that he picked me up, as if we are merely potential conquests, readily available.”
An interracial couple shared their experiences and the racist assumptions about them as a couple.

"Another element that has consistently shaped everything is the fact that this racism is imprinted, indeed, deeply embedded, in the very attitudes of the people themselves. It is the people who claim that the central problem facing Lima, or the country, is the presence of the Indians."
This testimony reflects on the racial politics in Peru, where indigenous and Black communities are seen as the worst in the racial social hierarchy.

"Based on my travel experience, I believe that, unfortunately, Peru is one of the most racist countries I know. I think the racism here against the cholo is worse or more visible than the racism against Black people. When it comes to Black people, it is more hidden, covert, and subtle.”
This testimony connects to what scholars have mentioned that racism in Peru is hidden.
"And so, I discover that, in the midst of this journey, learning how to be Black is difficult. It is hard, and at times, it is painful."
Susana's testimony reflects on what it means to be a Black woman in a racist society as Peru.

"This is what concerns us now: how do we create a different Peru for our children and grandchildren, one in which the suffering we have endured since our arrival 500 years ago disappears from this land once and for all? It is a difficult task, but we are working. We are fighting to ensure that, little by little, this becomes a country with a much more breathable atmosphere. A place where African people, their descendants, can live in harmony with others. and where we can finally recreate and live our culture with all the rights and significance it rightfully deserves. Ultimately, I have the right to be Black, and I have the right to feel good about being so. Democracy, in the end, means liberating the individual from the shackles that stifle and marginalize them. Thereby enabling their full self-realization. That is the work we are engaged in, and that is the path we will continue to follow.”
Jose “Pepe” Luciano reflects on the future of how to eradicate racism in Peru.
Images of Afro-Peruvian communal practices.
Afro-Peruvian music playing in the backgroud
